coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of silicon nanowire and crystalline carbon quantum dots heterostructure and study of photo response and photoluminescence property
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Sourav
Ray, Unmesha
Roy, Dipayan
Banerjee, Diptonil
Chattopadhyay, Kalyan Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Silicon nanowire based heterostrucure or nanocomposites becomes intrigued in next generation energy conversion devices. Herein, we have prepared n type silicon nanowires (n-SiNWs) and water soluble Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) through metal assisted etching and hydrothermal method respectively. The CQD possesses very low dimensions around 2–4 nm which becomes spin coated on n type silicon nanowires. The formation of silicon nanowires/ Carbon Quantum Dots heterostructures (n-SiNW/CQD) exhibits excellent photo current properties with Ilight/IDark had apparent value within the range 1.36–3.83. The heterostructure also exhibits a strong photoluminescence spectrum in blue region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo response
CQD
Si nanowire
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S2 magnified and (d) S3. XRD patterns of S1 and S3 are shown inset in Fig. 1 (a and d).
(a) FTIR (b) UV–Visible Diffuse Reflectance (C) Raman spectra of S1, S2 and S3. (d) Schematic diagram of n-SiNW/CQD hybrid structure.
I-V characteristic of (a) S1, (b) S2. Comparison of different (C) n-SiNW/CQD composite under (a) dark and (d)light illumination.
Synthesis strategy of carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) and n-SiNW/CQD heterostructure.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of silicon nanowire and crystalline carbon quantum dots heterostructure and study of photo response and photoluminescence property

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Nanotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon nanowire based heterostrucure or nanocomposites becomes intrigued in next generation energy conversion devices. Herein, we have prepared n type silicon nanowires (n-SiNWs) and water soluble Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) through metal assisted etching and hydrothermal method respectively. The CQD possesses very low dimensions around 2–4 nm which becomes spin coated on n type silicon nanowires. The formation of silicon nanowires/ Carbon Quantum Dots heterostructures (n-SiNW/CQD) exhibits excellent photo current properties with Ilight/IDark had apparent value within the range 1.36–3.83. The heterostructure also exhibits a strong photoluminescence spectrum in blue region.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of Carbon Nanotube, the interests of researchers on nanomaterials have increased due to the different properties of nanostructured materials [1]. Different nanostructures like two dimensional thin films, one dimensional nanowire, and zero-dimensional quantum dots (QDs) have been synthesized and explored for different applications [2–4]. Silicon nanowires (SiNWs) have been reportedly used in many applications like in FETs, sensors, and photocatalytic applications [5–7]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the other have also been used in applications like electrodes, light emitting diodes and cell, tissue imaging [8–10]. Among all the mentioned classifications of nanomaterials, SiNWs has diameter in the range of 1–100 nm and lengths ranging from few micrometers to few centimeters like while quantum dots have dimension within 10 nm. Silicon is considered to be abundant and electronic devices having good thermal and electrical stability. Due to these reasons, silicon is used in most of the electronic circuits and microelectronic devices [11]. But silicon has an indirect bandgap which restricts its use in optoelectronics applications though most of the electronic devices are made with silicon. To overcome this problem, special forms of silicon like silicon nanowires (SiNWs), porous silicon and silicon nanocrystalline, etc are being studied extensively for their possible application in optoelectronic and other devices [12–14].

Herein we report the synthesis of n type silicon nanowires (n-SiNWs) through metal assisted etching and water soluble CQD through hydrothermal synthesis process respectively. Later three hybrid structures (n-SiNW-CQD) were prepared by covering the n-SiNWs with the CQDs with different synthesis time and synthesis temperature, using spin coating process (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared materials have shown excellent photo response and PL properties.

From the FESEM images the standing vertical structures of the as synthesized n-SiNWs few hundred nanometers has been confirmed (Fig. 1 a-c). This vertical structure is extremely helpful in excellent light absorption and photon management [15]. It is reported that the length of the n-SiNWs can be increased if the etching can be done for few hours. Fig. 1d implies the XRD data of all samples. From XRD analysis a sharp peak near 2θ ~ 700 of S1 and S3 confirms the presence of Si [16].

HRTEM images of the as prepared CQD sample are shown in Figure S-1. It can be seen that the CQDs are well dispersed within the solution with an apparent spherical nature. The CQDs in sample S1 has dimension in the range of 2–4 nm. However, with increasing the temperature, the size of QDs become enhances which is probably due to the absence of surfactants and the agglomeration of QDs. The dimension of CQDs of sample S2 to S3 increases to a value 6 nm and 8 nm respectively.

Fig. 2 shows the UV–Vis reflectance spectra where a gradual decrease in the reflectance from 200 to 1000 nm of all samples, whereas the reflectance becomes increase in1000- 2200 nm range. As the reflectance decreases, absorption increases and thus these materials can be used as an antireflection coating. Fig. 2a depicts the FTIR spectra in absorption mode of all the three samples taken in the wavenumber range 250– 4500 cm−1 with the resolution of 4 cm−1. The band between 462 and 495 cm1 is due to the formation of SiO2 on silicon wafer. The peak at 600 cm−1, 900 cm-1and 1100 cm−1, 2930 cm−1 signifies the presence of Si-O-Si bond, Si-H bond, C-O-C and C–H bond respectively [22]. The band between 1400 and 1850 cm−1 is due to CH, C–C and C = O bond. The band between 3000 and 3500 cm-1is due to the formation of –OH bond due to the functionalization of water soluble CQDs by –OH groups [22]. Fig. 2c implies Raman spectra where D and G band occurs near 1350 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1 respectively which concludes the presence of CQD in the system with defects.

From Fig. 3 it can be clearly seen that S1 and S2 samples showed good photoreponse with an appreciable increment of current after exposure of visible light. The increment of current for sample S3 have shown some noise like variation under dark and illumination conditions. The n-SiNW and CQDs forming a heterostructure may have caused the gradual increment of the current in the illuminated condition [18].

Vertical nature of as synthesized n-SiNWs and the unique photon management (PM) characteristics of the SiNWs can also be considered responsible for the exciting photocurrent property of the hybrid samples [16]. The values of Ilight/IDark were determined for a particular voltage for all the three samples with an apparent value within the range 1.36–3.83 (Table S1). The excitation introduced by the visible light photon and the photon management property of the as synthesized n-SiNW may be accounted for by the observed photoresponse property.

Photoluminescence property of all the n-SiNW-CQD hybrid samples has also been studied in figure S-2. Generally, a broad PL peak centered around 750 nm is reported for porous silicon nanowire which can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 752 and 688 nm [19]. Generally, SiNWs has grown without catalyst having broad PL emission composed of five bands- a UV band centered at 350 nm, two blue bands centered at 465 nm and 482 nm, and two green bands centered at 502 nm and 526 nm [20,22]. Tuan et al. reported PL from SiNW with core–shell structures having weak UV emission band peaked at 390 nm compared to another 645-nm red band [21]. In our study, all the heterostructure shows a broad emission peak at blue region (fig S-2a). In fig.S-2a a broad peak at 389.17 nm occurs for S1 under the excitation (λex) of 243.8 nm and with increasing the excitation wavelength to 260 nm, the emission peak exhibits at 398.40 nm. Similarly, For S2 and S3, the emission peak occurs at 389.8 nm and 389 nm respectively at the excitation wavelength 243.8 nm. In our study a slight blue shift occurs mainly the presence of different trap states, and different polyromantic fluorophores with the skeleton of carbon [23]. Generally, the PL mechanism of SiO2 is mainly due to oxygen core, functionalities, deficiency, and defects states. Depending on the nature of oxygen coordination (one, two, three coordination towards silicon) various luminescence bands from 1.9 eV (652 nm) to 5 eV (250 nm) have been observed [24–26]. However, PL profile of SiO2 is strongly layered dependent where intensity enhances with increasing the layer thickness [27]. During silicon nanowire (SiNW) synthesis, the formation of SiO2 on silicon wafer reveals lower impact towards PL profile which has been shown in Fig S3. The PL spectra of bare silicon nanowire (SiNW) have been determined at 244 nm (5 eV) excitation wavelength and compare it with n-SiNW/CQD heterostructure (S1). It has been observed the intensity of S1 is a few hundred times more intense compare to SiNW suggests SiO2 has negligible effect on PL performance.

In this work, we have reported a very simple metal assisted chemical etching route to synthesize vertically aligned silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and CQD heterostructure using n type silicon wafer. The enhancement in current under light illumination occurs in all heterostructures. However, a strong photoluminescence spectrum under blue-greenish region becomes the key features of all n-SiNW/CQD heterostructure. Thus, the material has shown excellent photodetector and photoluminescence (PL) properties and can be a potential applicant in the fields of biosensing and optoelectronic integrated circuits. [17]

Sourav Sarkar: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. : . Unmesha Ray: Formal analysis, Investigation. Dipayan Roy: Writing - original draft. Diptonil Banerjee: Methodology. Kalyan Kumar Chattopadhyay: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors, SS and DR wish to acknowledge the Science & Engineering Research Board (SERB), a statutory body of Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India (File No. EEQ/2018/001127) for providing financial support towards the implementation of the project along with the fellowship..

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.130493.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
